title: Insider Trading? $320 Million Bet on Oil Plunge Just Minutes Before Trump's Iran Announcement
description: Oil markets saw a massive, $320 million spike in short bets minutes before US President Donald Trump announced a delay in strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure.
lede: Hundreds of millions of dollars were wagered on a sharp drop in oil prices just 15 minutes before US President Donald Trump announced a postponement of strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure. Market analysts and observers are now raising serious questions about potential insider trading leading up to the unexpected decision.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: At 06:49 Eastern Time on Monday, an unusual flurry of activity hit the New York Mercantile Exchange. Traders placed 733 bets on WTI crude oil contracts. Within one minute, that figure surged to 2,007 bets—totaling approximately $170 million.

A nearly identical surge occurred simultaneously with Brent crude contracts. Trading volume skyrocketed from a mere 20 to over 1,600 trades within a minute, representing around $150 million. According to market data reviewed by the BBC, these specific time slots on previous Mondays typically see a fraction of this volume.

Fifteen minutes later, at 07:04 ET, President Trump took to Truth Social to announce that Washington had held 'VERY GOOD AND PRODUCTIVE CONVERSATIONS' with Tehran. Following the post, oil prices plummeted by 14% to $84 a barrel, guaranteeing massive payouts for the traders who bet against the market.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The timing of these high-stakes trades points directly toward the possibility of individuals profiting illegally from advanced knowledge of US foreign policy decisions. A White House spokesman has since told the Financial Times that the administration does not tolerate officials 'illegally profiteering off of insider knowledge.'

Mukesh Sahdev, chief oil analyst at XAnalysts, called the trading volume 'abnormal,' noting there were absolutely no prior public indications that serious talks between the US and Iran were underway. Betting heavily on oil dropping under those market conditions defies standard trading logic without insider information.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The oil market had been on a knife-edge. Only two days prior, on Saturday, Trump threatened to 'obliterate' Iranian power plants if the Strait of Hormuz—a crucial chokepoint for 20% of the world's oil and gas—was not reopened within 48 hours. Markets had already fallen sharply across Asia in anticipation of an escalation.

This is not an isolated incident of US foreign policy overlapping with highly profitable, uniquely timed betting. In January, wagers surged on the prediction platform Polymarket regarding the ousting of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro just hours before he was seized by American forces, netting one bettor more than $436,000.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Regulatory scrutiny is mounting. The BBC has confirmed it contacted both the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission and the UK's Financial Conduct Authority regarding the suspicious trading patterns.

Meanwhile, the geopolitical landscape remains volatile. Later on Monday, the Iranian government outright denied that any talks had occurred, with parliament speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf dismissing the US claims as 'fake news' designed to manipulate financial markets. Following Iran's denial, Asian stock markets began creeping up again on Tuesday.
